school: Leading rabbi of Medina who recognized Muhammad as the prophet foretold in the Torah; one of the Companions whom the Prophet publicly confirmed would be in Paradise; his conversion had a profound impact on Medinan Jewish-Muslim relations
quotes: When I first saw the face of the Prophet, I knew it was not the face of a liar.
O people, spread peace, feed the hungry, maintain family ties, and pray at night when people are asleep — and you will enter Paradise in peace.

school: Among the first five people to accept Islam; the Prophet declared: 'The earth has not carried nor the sky covered a more truthful man than Abu Dharr'; fiercely opposed the accumulation of wealth; exiled by Uthman to Rabadha for his social critiques; patron saint of Muslim reformers and socialists
parents: Junadah ibn Sakan / Ramla bint al-Waqi'ya al-Ghaffariyya
spouses: Umm Dhur
quotes: O poor man, do not grieve your poverty — many a rich man will wish on the Day of Resurrection that his skin had been cut with scissors.
I advise you to fear God in secret and in public, and to speak truth even when it is bitter.
The cure for ignorance is to ask.

school: Persian Companion of the Prophet — searched for truth across Zoroastrianism and Christianity before reaching Islam; the Prophet declared 'Salman is from us, the Ahl al-Bayt'; patron of Persian Sufism
parents: Yasir ibn 'Amir / Sumayyah bint Khayyat
children: Muhammad bin 'Ammar bin Yasir
quotes: I have lived long enough to see that the world is not worth the wing of a mosquito in the sight of God.
Three things grieve me: parting from my brothers, the sight of poverty in a believer, and a Quran that is not acted upon.
Live in this world as a traveller and take from it only your provision.

school: The Prophet's camel stopped at his house upon arrival in Medina — making Abu Ayyub the prophet's first host; fought in dozens of expeditions; died during the Muslim siege of Constantinople aged over 100 and was buried beneath its walls; his tomb near Istanbul is still a pilgrimage site
parents: Zayd ibn Kulayb / Hind bint Sa'eed b. 'Amr
children: 'Amra bint Abi Ayoub
spouses: Umm Ayoub bint Qays bin Sa'd
quotes: I will never turn back from fighting in the cause of God until I meet Him.
What prevented me from going out to fight was not old age but the thought that I might be a burden to the Muslims.

school: Ansari companion known for a profound episode of repentance — when he inadvertently betrayed the Prophet's confidence regarding the Banu Qurayza, he tied himself to a mosque pillar vowing not to untie himself until God forgave him; the Quran references this repentance

Freed slave who served the Prophet since before his mission; was gifted to the Prophet by his uncle Abbas; freed upon converting to Islam; his family became guardians of the Prophet's household after his death; transmitted many hadiths about the Prophet's daily life

His parents Yasir and Sumayya were the first martyrs of Islam, tortured to death for their faith; Ammar himself was tortured and permitted to utter words of unbelief while his heart remained firm — the revelation of taqiyya; helped build the first mosque; sided with Ali and killed at Siffin
parents: Jahash bin Ri'ab / Umayma bint 'Abd al-Muttalib
children: Muhammad bin 'Abdullah bin Jahsh
spouses: Zaynab bint Khazeemah , or, Fatima bint Abi Hubaysh
quotes: Whoever fasts from the doubts of the world shall break his fast in the gardens of Paradise.
Paradise is under the shade of swords.
We were promised difficulty in this path — and here it is.

school: A slave who was among the first seven people to publicly declare Islam; tortured severely by his masters — red-hot coals placed on his back; the Prophet could not yet relieve him but promised victory; later praised the early Muslims' endurance before later ease of life; died in Kufa
parents: al-Aratt b. Jandalah
children: 'Abdullah bin Khabbab ibn al-Aratt , 'Abdur Rahman bin Khabbab ibn al-Aratt , Zaynab bint Khabbab ibn al-Aratt
spouses: Mulayka wife Khabbab
quotes: We complained to the Messenger of God while he was resting in the shade of the Kaaba, and he said: those who came before you were sawed in half, yet that did not turn them from their faith.
We emigrated with the Prophet seeking the face of God — and our reward fell due upon God.

school: Ansari poet-warrior who composed praise of the Prophet; one of three commanders at the Battle of Mu'ta — martyred after Zayd ibn Haritha and Ja'far ibn Abi Talib fell; the Prophet wept at news of his death
First muezzin of Islam — Abyssinian slave freed by Abu Bakr; his voice announcing the first adhan is one of the most iconic moments in Islamic history
parents: Ribah / Hamamah
quotes: One, One — meaning God is One.
I cannot bear to see the morning without having heard the call to prayer.
My master is God, and Muhammad is the Messenger of God.

school: One of the wealthiest Companions and among the Ashra Mubashshara; emigrated to Medina with nothing and rebuilt his fortune through trade; donated vast sums to the early Muslim community; led the burial prayer over Umar ibn al-Khattab
parents: 'Awf b. 'Abd 'Awf b. 'Abd b. al-Harith / al-Shifa' bint 'Awf
children: Salim, Muhammad bin 'Abdur-Rahman , Ibrahim bin 'Abdur Rahman , Humaid bin 'Abdur Rahman , Isma'il, Hamida, Ammatur Rahman, Ma'n, 'Umar bin 'Abdur Rahman bin Awf , Zayd bin 'Abdur Rahman , Ammatur Rahman, 'Urwa (Akbar) bin 'Abdur Rahman , Salim(Asghar) bin 'Abdur Rahman , Abu Bakr bin 'Abdur Rahman , 'Abdullah bin 'Abdur Rahman , Abu Salama bin 'Abdur Rahman , 'Abdur Rahman bin 'Abdur Rahman , Mus'ub bin 'Abdur Rahman , Suhayl, Jawayriya
spouses: Umm Kulthum bint 'Utba b. Rabi'a , Umm Kulthum bint 'Uqba , Sahla bint 'Asim bin 'Ady , Bahria bint Hani, Sehlah bint Suhayl , Umm Hakim bint Qariz bin Khalid , bint Abi al-Husayn, Tumadir bint al-Asbagh Kalbia , Asma' bint Salama/Makhrabah , Umm Wald, Badiyya bint Ghilan al-Thaqafi
quotes: I fear that I may be called to account for my wealth — for it is easier to be patient in poverty than to give thanks for abundance.
The best trade is the trade of the Hereafter.

school: Son of the legendary generous Arab Hatim al-Ta'i; converted to Islam and became a loyal companion; one of the longest-lived Companions (over 100 years old); remained steadfastly loyal to Ali; famous for his father's pre-Islamic generosity becoming a touchstone of Arab virtue

Ansari warrior at Badr and Uhud; at Uhud his eye was struck out and hung on his cheek — the Prophet placed it back with his hand and it became the better of his two eyes; this remains one of the best-attested miracles of the Prophet

Christian monk-scholar from Palestine who converted to Islam and became a Companion; introduced the practice of hanging lamps in mosques; famous for narrating the story of al-Jassasa and the Dajjal — one of the few hadiths the Prophet verified in public; pioneered nightly prayer vigils (tahajjud) worship
parents: Aus b. Kharija b. Suwad
quotes: Religion is sincerity — sincerity to God, to His Book, to His Messenger, and to the leaders and common people of the Muslims.
The Prophet said about me: Tamim told me something that agrees with what I had told you.

school: The official orator-spokesman of the Prophet; when Quran 49:2 warned against raising one's voice above the Prophet's, he feared himself damned and hid — the Prophet assured him: 'You are not of the hypocrites, you will live with praise and die a martyr'; died heroically at the Battle of Yamama against Musaylima
parents: Thabit bin Qays b. Shammas / Jamila bint 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy
children: 'Abdullah, Suliaman, Yahya, Isma'il bin Muhammad bin Thabit , Yusuf bin Muhammad bin Thabit , Others
spouses: Umm 'Abdullah bint Hafs of B.Harith b.al-Khazraj, Others

school: The Prophet's chief scribe and the foremost reciter of the Quran — the Prophet said: 'The best reciter among my Companions is Ubayy ibn Ka'b'; one of four Companions whose Quran recitation was recommended; the Prophet personally told him: 'God has commanded me to recite to you'; his variant mushaf was kept alongside the Uthmanic one
parents: Rawahah bin Tha'labah / Kabsha bint Waqid bin 'Amr
quotes: The Prophet told me that God commanded me to recite the Quran to you — and I wept from joy that God had mentioned my name.
Whoever reads the Quran seeking only the face of God and understanding of the religion, his reward is equal to that of the angels.

school: Chief of the Khazraj tribe of Medina and leader of the Ansar; a candidate for the caliphate at the Saqifa assembly after the Prophet's death; withdrew rather than swear allegiance to Abu Bakr; died in Syria (some say shot by jinn); his kitchen fed 80 people daily
parents: 'Ubadah ibn Dulaim bin Haritha / 'Amra bint Mas'ud #4
children: Sa'id bin Sa'd ibn 'Ubadah , Ishaq bin Sa'd ibn 'Ubadah , Muhammad, 'Abdur Rahman, Qays ibn Sa'd ibn 'Ubadah , Umamah, Sudas
spouses: Ghaziyya bint Sa'd ibn Khalifa , Fukayha bint 'Ubayd b. Dulaim
quotes: The Ansar are my people, and we will shelter the Prophet as we shelter our own families.
We pledged to protect the Messenger of God as we protect our women and children.

school: Called 'Trustee of the Nation' by the Prophet; conqueror of Syria; commander who took Damascus and Jerusalem; died in the plague of Amwas in Syria; one of the Ashra Mubashshara promised Paradise
parents: 'Abdullah ibn al-Jarrah / Umm Ghanm bint Jabir bin 'Abdul-'Uzza
children: Yazid and 'Ubaidah, 'Umair
spouses: Hind bint Jabar, Warja
quotes: We were the most abased of peoples — then God honoured us with Islam. If we seek honour in anything other than what God honoured us with, He will abase us again.
The commander who loves his men and his men love him has already won half the battle.

school: One of twelve delegates who swore the first Pledge of Aqaba; taught the people of Medina Quran and Islam before the Hijra; first judge appointed over Palestine; taught the Companions in Syria; transmitted many important hadiths including the foundational pledge of allegiance to the Prophet
quotes: We pledged allegiance to the Prophet: not to associate anything with God, not to steal, not to commit adultery, not to kill our children, and not to disobey him in any good.
The first thing taken from this nation will be humility and the last thing will be prayer.

school: One of the first six people to accept Islam; said to be the first person to fight on horseback for Islam; at Badr he declared unwavering loyalty — 'We will not say what Banu Isra'il said to Moses; we will fight to your right and left!'; the Prophet praised him lavishly
parents: Zayd ibn Qays al-Khazraji / Mahabba bint Waqid bin 'Amr
children: Bilal bin Abi al-Drda'a , 2 other sons
spouses: Umm al-Darda , Mahabba bint ar-Rabi' ibn 'Amr
quotes: O Messenger of God, we will not say to you what the Children of Israel said to Moses — go you and your Lord and fight. Rather, we say: go you and your Lord and fight, and we shall fight alongside you.
By God, if you were to lead us to the furthest lands, we would follow you.

school: Chief of Banu Abd al-Ashhal; his entire tribe converted to Islam through his influence — the first tribe to accept Islam collectively; famous for his beautiful Quran recitation during which horses and angels gathered to listen; the Prophet called him among the best men of Medina
quotes: I never felt a sweeter night than the night I spent reciting the Quran in my courtyard — and the angels descended with lights.
By God, we will not leave the Messenger of God until we have fought around him.

school: A merchant who converted and left business for full devotion to knowledge; judge of Damascus; famed for philosophical wisdom — 'I marvel at the man who works for his body which will perish, but neglects his soul which will not'; patron of early Syrian asceticism
parents: Zayd ibn Qays al-Khazraji / Mahabba bint Waqid bin 'Amr
children: Bilal bin Abi al-Drda'a , 2 other sons
spouses: Umm al-Darda , Mahabba bint ar-Rabi' ibn 'Amr
quotes: Whoever considers his speech to be among his deeds will speak little except about that which concerns him.
If you only knew what awaits you after death, you would not eat food with appetite nor drink water with thirst, and you would go out to the open fields beating your chests.
Shall I not tell you the best and purest of your deeds? The remembrance of God.

school: Born in the Byzantine world (possibly Mesopotamia); enslaved as a child and taken to Mecca; among the first to accept Islam; when he emigrated to Medina, Quraysh tried to stop him — he offered all his wealth and they let him pass; the Quran (2:207) was revealed in his honor: 'of men who sell their soul for God's pleasure'
parents: al-'As ibn Wa'il / Layla bint Harmalah
children: 'Abdullah bin 'Amr bin al-'Aas
spouses: Rayta bint Munabbih bin al-Hajjaj , Umm Kulthum bint 'Uqba

school: Husband of Umm Sulaym; the greatest Ansari archer who shielded the Prophet at Uhud; the Prophet declared his shield arm was worth more than an army; famous for the hadith of sacrificing his beloved garden for the sake of God
quotes: I heard the Prophet say that the angels do not enter a house in which there is a dog or a picture — so I shall remove every obstacle between my home and the angels.
I love the garden, but I love God more — so I give it away as charity.

school: Among the Companions who transmitted the famous Ghadir Khumm hadith ('Whoever considers me his master, Ali is also his master'); also reported the Prophet's saying about two weighty things — the Quran and his Household; settled in Kufa; transmitted many hadiths; lost his eyesight in old age
parents: Arqam b. Zayd b. Qays

school: Son of Zayd ibn Amr (who searched for true monotheism before Islam); one of the first to accept Islam; among the Ashra Mubashshara promised Paradise; participated in early battles and conquests of Syria
parents: Zayd bin 'Amr bin Nufayl / Fatima bint Ba'aja
children: Asma bint Sa'id bin Zayd , Zayd, Hisham bin S'aid bin Zayd , 'Abdur Rahman
spouses: Fatima bint al-Khattab , Khayra bint Qays
quotes: I have lived to see the day when the very ground from which Islam grew has become a garden — and I was there when it was only a seed.
The blessing of companionship with the Prophet is enough for any life.

school: The Prophet's personal servant and sandal-bearer; the Prophet said his Quran recitation was the most beautiful; founder of the Kufa school of law
parents: Mas'ud b. Ghafil b. Habib / Umm 'Abdullah bin Mas'ud
children: 'Abdur Rahman bin 'Abdullah , Abu 'Ubaida bin 'Abdullah bin Mas'ud
spouses: Rayta bint 'Abdullah , Zaynab bint. Abi Mu'awiya
quotes: Acquire knowledge and impart it to the people — for knowledge does not diminish by being shared.
The most truthful speech is the Book of Allah; the best guidance is the guidance of Muhammad.
Follow and do not innovate — for you have been given enough.
The Quran was not revealed to be recited quickly — it was revealed to be acted upon.
Follow the traces of those who have gone before — do not innovate, for what you need has already been given.
Knowledge is not the abundance of narration — knowledge is the fear of God.
The Quran was revealed to be acted upon — do not make the recitation your only concern.

school: One of the Ashra Mubashshara — the ten Companions guaranteed Paradise by the Prophet; shielded the Prophet at Uhud with his own body, losing use of his fingers; one of the six members of the Shura council for the caliphate
parents: 'Ubaidullah b. 'Uthman b. 'Amr / as-Sa'bah bint al-Hadrami
children: Muhammad As-Sajjad ibn Talha , 'Imran ibn Talha , Musa ibn Talha , Ya'qub ibn Talha , Isma'il ibn Talha , Ishaq ibn Talha , Zakariyya bin Talha , Yusuf ibn Talha , 'Aisha bint Talha , 'Isa bin Talha bin 'Ubaidullah , Yahya ibn Talha , Umm Ishaq
spouses: Hamnah bint Jahsh , Khawla bint al-Qa'Qa' , Umm Aban bint 'Utba b. Rabi'a , Umm Khultum bint Abi Bakr , Suada bint 'Auf al-Murriya , al-Jarba' bint Qasama al-Tayyi'
quotes: I heard the Prophet say that for every prophet there is a disciple — and my disciple is Zubayr.
On the Day of Uhud, I had no concern except to shield the Messenger of God.

school: Nephew of Khadija and cousin of Ali; the Prophet called him 'my disciple'; fifth person to accept Islam; among the bravest of the early Muslims; killed at the Battle of the Camel
parents: Awwam ibn Khuwaylid bin Asad bin 'Abdul-'Uzza / Safiyya bint Abd al-Muttalib
children: 'Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr , al-Mundhir bin al-Zubayr , 'Urwa ibn al-Zubayr , ''Asim ibn al-Zubayr , al-Muhajir ibn al-Zubayr , Khadija al-Kubra bint al-Zubayr , Ummul Hasan bint al-Zubayr , 'Aisha bint al-Zubayr , Khalid bin al-Zubayr , 'Amr bin al-Zubayr , Habibah bint al-Zubayr , Sawda bint al-Zubayr , Hind bint al-Zubayr , Zainab ibn al-Zubayr , Mus'ab ibn al-Zubayr , Hamza bin al-Zubayr , Ramla bint al-Zubayr , 'Ubaida bin al-Zubayr , Ja'far bin al-Zubayr , Hafsa bint al-Zubayr , Khadija al-Sughra bint al-Zubayr
spouses: Asma' bint Abi Bakr , Umm Khalid bin Khalid ibn Sa'id , Umm Kulthum bint 'Uqba , al-Rabab bnt Unaif, Zainab bint Bishr, al-Halal bint Qays, Tumadir bint al-Asbagh Kalbia
quotes: Fight for the truth, even if you must fight alone.
I never set out for a battle except that I felt the ground shake beneath me from eagerness.

school: The first to shed blood for Islam in battle; commander at the decisive Battle of Qadisiyyah that broke Sassanid Persia; founder of the garrison city of Kufa; one of the Ashra Mubashshara and the Shura council of six
parents: Malik bin Uhaib bin 'Abd Manaf / Hamnah bint Sufyan bin Umayyah bin 'Abd Shams
children: Ishaq bin Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas , Umm Hakam, 'Umar bin Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas , Muhammad bin Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas , Umm Qasim, Umm Khultum, 'Amir bin Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas , Isma'il bin Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas , Mus'ub bin Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas , Ibrahim bin Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas , Ya'qub bin Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas , 'Aisha bint Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas , Yahya bin Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas , Salih, 'Abdullah bin Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas , 'Abdur Rahman, 'Umayr bin Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas , 'Amr bin Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas , Others
spouses: bint Shihab bin 'Abdullah, Maria bint Qays, Umm 'Amir, Umm Hakim bint Qariz bin Khalid , Others
quotes: By God, I was the first Arab to shoot an arrow in the cause of God.
I am content with whatever God decrees for me — for His decree is better than my choice.

school: Settled in Syria and became the last surviving Companion there; known for extensive hadith transmission and for his asceticism; the Prophet specially prayed for his tribe (Bahila); transmitted 250 hadiths
parents: 'Ajalan b. Wahb b. 'Amr
quotes: Read the Quran, for it will come on the Day of Judgment as an intercessor for its companions.
The Prophet said: the best of what a believer can do after the obligations is to seek knowledge.

school: One of three Companions who failed to join the Prophet's expedition to Tabuk without excuse; their repentance and God's acceptance is recorded in the Quran (9:118); a distinguished poet who composed verses defending Islam; his eyesight later failed
Ansari companion sent alone on a mission by the Prophet to neutralize a tribal leader gathering forces against Medina; known for his valor and obedience; transmitted hadiths and settled in Egypt

One of the finest horsemen among the Companions; called 'the horseman of Allah's Messenger'; fought in most of the Prophet's major expeditions; transmitted 170 hadiths; famous for his gentle treatment of his family
quotes: When I ride into battle, I think of nothing but meeting God.
The Prophet told me: may God protect your face as you protected His Messenger’s face.

school: Participated in many battles with the Prophet; settled in Kufa where he became a primary narrator of hadiths about the Prophet's military campaigns; among the last Companions alive in Kufa; transmitted important hadiths about prayer and angels
parents: Azib bin al-Harith / Umm Habiba bint Abi Habiba/Umm Khalid bint Thabit
children: Yazid bin al-Bra'a bin Aazb , 'Ubaid bin al-Bra'a bin Aazb , Yunus, 'Azib, Yahya bin al-Bra'a bin Aazb , Umm 'Abdullah, al-Rabi' bin al-Bara' bin Azib , Ibrahim bin al-Bara' bin 'Aazib
quotes: We used to pray behind the Prophet, and when he said ‘God hears the one who praises Him,’ none of us would bend his back in prostration until the Prophet had placed his forehead on the ground.
Not all of us who pledged allegiance at Hudaybiyyah were at Badr — but those who were at Badr were the best of us.

school: Famous for his cunning diplomacy — the Persian court called him al-Dahi (the cunning one); governor of both Basra and Kufa under Umar and later Muawiya; participated in the conquest of Iraq and Iran; famous for negotiations with foreign powers
parents: Shu'ba bin Abi 'Amir / Asma' bint al-Afqam of B.Dahman b. Nasr
children: 'Urwa bin al-Mughira bin Shu'ba , Hamza bin al-Mughira bin Shu'ba , Ya'fur bin al-Mughira bin Shu'ba , 'Aqqar bin al-Mughira bin Shu'ba
spouses: Zaynab/Maymuna bint Abi Sufyan , Zaynab bint Uthman bin Maz'un

school: Yemeni companion praised by the Prophet for his beautiful Quran recitation — 'he has been given a flute from the flutes of David'; governor of Basra and Kufa under Umar; played a controversial role as arbitrator at the Battle of Siffin
parents: 'Abdullah ibn Qays bin Saleem / Zabiya bint Wahb
children: Ibrahim bin Abi Musa al-Asha'ari , Abu Bakr bin Abi Musa al-Asha'ari , Abu Barda bin Abi Musa al-Asha'ari , Musa bin Abi Musa al-Asha'ari
spouses: Umm Khultum bint Fadl ibn al-'Abbas , Umm 'Abdullah bint Abi Duma
quotes: The example of a believer who recites the Quran is like an orange — its fragrance is pleasant and its taste is pleasant.
A believer to another believer is like a building whose parts support one another.

school: The most prolific hadith transmitter — 5,374 recorded narrations; spent 3 years day and night with the Prophet
parents: / Umaima/Maymuna
children: Daughter
spouses: Basra bint Ghazwan
quotes: I have preserved two vessels of knowledge from the Messenger of God — one I have shared; the other, if I shared it, this throat would be cut.
The pursuit of knowledge is a duty upon every Muslim.
Take care of your duty to God and you will be the most devoted of men.
Renounce the world and God will love you — renounce what others have and people will love you.
I memorised two vessels of knowledge from the Messenger of God — one I have shared. The other, if I shared it, they would cut my throat.
The strong man is not the one who wrestles — the strong man is the one who controls himself when angry.
Renew your faith. They asked: How? He said: Say La ilaha ill Allah often.
The strong man is not the one who wrestles — the strong man is the one who controls himself when angry.
Renew your faith by saying La ilaha ill Allah often.
Seek knowledge from the cradle to the grave.

school: The Prophet said: 'The most learned of my community in halal and haram is Muadh ibn Jabal'; Governor of Yemen; died in the plague of Amwas
parents: 'Abd al-Muttalib b. Hashim b. 'Abd Manaf / Halah bint Wuhayb al-Zuhriyya
children: 'Umama bint Hamza , 'Umara bin Hamza , Ya'la bin Hamza , Fatima bin Hamza , Ammtullah bint Hamza
spouses: Salma bint 'Umays , Khawla bint Qays bin Qahd
quotes: Learn knowledge, for learning it for Allah's sake is reverence, seeking it is worship, revising it is glorification, and teaching it is charity.
Sit with the scholars, listen to the wise, and make it your habit to enter the houses of knowledge.

school: Entrusted with the names of the hypocrites (munafiqun) — a secret the Prophet shared with no one else; people would secretly ask him 'Am I among the hypocrites?'; governor of Ctesiphon; a key figure in early Sufi chains as the 'keeper of inner knowledge'

Companion who reported hearing angels greet him until he cauterized a wound (treating an illness), after which he no longer heard them; judge and teacher of Basra; known for remarkable piety; transmitted important hadiths about qada and qadar (divine decree)
quotes: Modesty brings nothing but good.
I was with the Prophet in many campaigns, and I never saw anything more beloved to him than prayer.

school: Said to be one of the last Companions to die in Medina; witnessed the Prophet's era as a young boy and transmitted hadiths about the Prophet's household, prayers, and daily practices; lived over 90 years preserving the memory of early Islam
parents: Jabal ibn 'Amr b. Aus / Hind bint Sahl al-Juhayna
children: Umm 'Abdullah bint Mu'adh ibn Jabal , 'Abdur Rahman bin Mu'adh ibn Jabal
spouses: Umm 'Amr bint Khalid b. 'Amr of B.Sawad B.Salima

school: Called 'the Yusuf of this umma' by the Prophet for his striking beauty; led the mission to destroy Dhu al-Khalasa (the last Arab pagan idol sanctuary in Yemen); a tribal chief of Bajila who brought his entire tribe into Islam; transmitted 100+ hadiths

Son of the second Caliph Umar; one of the most prolific hadith narrators (2,630 narrations); known for meticulously following the Prophet's Sunnah in the smallest details; refused political positions to dedicate himself to scholarship
parents: 'Umar ibn al-Khattab / Zaynab bint Maz'un
children: Abu Bakr, Abu 'Ubaida, Waqid bin 'Abdullah ibn 'Umar , 'Abdullah bin 'Abdullah Ibn 'Umar , 'Umar bin 'Abdullah Ibn 'Umar , Hafsa, Sawda, Bilal bin 'Abdullah bin 'Umar , Zayd bin 'Abdullah bin 'Umar , Salim bin 'Abdullah bin 'Umar , 'Ubaidullah bin 'Abdullah bin 'Umar , Hamza bin 'Abdullah bin 'Umar
spouses: Safiyya bint Abi 'Ubaid al-Thaqafi , Umm Walad, Umm Walad

school: Married on the eve of Uhud and martyred the very next morning; because he had no time to perform ghusl before battle, the Prophet declared the angels washed his body — one of the most poignant martyrdom narratives in early Islamic tradition

Companion who became acting governor of Basra; known for strict, sometimes harsh governance; transmitted numerous hadiths; one of the last Companions alive in Iraq; had a famous competition with Rafi ibn Khadij over who could throw arrows farther in the Prophet's presence

Zayd ibn Thabit (c. 611–665), the Prophet's primary scribe and the man charged with compiling the Quran into a single written text. First under Abu Bakr, then under Uthman when he produced the standardised text.
parents: Thabit b. ad-Dahhak b. Zayd / al-Nuwwar bint Malik bin Sirma
children: Sa'd ibn Zayd ibn Thabit , Kharija ibn Zayd ibn Thabit , Suliaman ibn Zayd ibn Thabit , Yahya ibn Zayd ibn Thabit , Isma'il ibn Zayd ibn Thabit , Umm 'Uthman, Umm Zayd, Saleet bin Zayd Ibn Thabit , 'Abdur Rahman bin Zayd Ibn Thabit , 'Abdullah bin Zayd Ibn Thabit , Zayd bin Zayd Ibn Thabit
spouses: Jamila bint Sa'd ibn ar-Rabi' , Fatima bint al-Mujallal , Umm Walad
quotes: When we collected the Quran, I feared that if we did not gather it, a portion of it might be lost forever.
I have never found anything more difficult than collecting the Quran — if they had asked me to move a mountain, it would have been easier.

school: Anas ibn Malik (612–712), who served as the Prophet's personal attendant for ten years from the age of ten. One of the most prolific hadith narrators, transmitting over 2,286 hadiths.
parents: Malik ibn Nadr / Umm Sulaym bint Milhan
children: al-Nadr bin Anas bin Malik , 'Abdullah bin Anas bin Malik , Abu Bakr bin Anas bin Malik , Musa bin Anas bin Malik
quotes: I served the Prophet for ten years and he never once said to me: Why did you do that? or Why did you not do that?
The prayer of the traveller is a mercy — God has lightened your burden.
Whoever loves to meet God, God loves to meet him.

school: One of the most prolific hadith transmitters among the Ansar; his father was martyred at Uhud; participated in many expeditions including Khandaq; known for transmitting hadiths related to the Day of Judgment, the unseen, and moral conduct
parents: Malik bin Sinan / Unaysa bint 'Amr bin Qays
children: 'Abdur Rahman bin Abi Sa'id al-Khudri , S'aid bin Abi S'aid al-Khudri
spouses: Zaynab bint Ka'b bin 'Ajra al-Ansariyya
quotes: Whoever among you sees an evil, let him change it with his hand — if he cannot, then with his tongue — if he cannot, then with his heart, and that is the weakest of faith.
The believer’s vision does not lie — for his vision comes from God’s light.
Guard your tongue — it is the most dangerous part of you.
Whoever among you sees an evil, let him change it with his hand — if he cannot, then with his tongue — if he cannot, then with his heart, and that is the weakest of faith.
The believer’s vision does not lie — for his vision comes from God’s light.
Guard your tongue — it is the most dangerous part of you.

school: Companions of the Prophet — one of the most important transmitters of hadith; authority on the Prophet's private life and character
parents: Abu Bakr As-Siddique / Umm Ruman
spouses: Prophet Muhammad(saw)
quotes: Acquire knowledge before you become a leader — for once you lead, you will find no time to learn.
The people most severely tested are the prophets, then those nearest to them, then those nearest to them.
Whoever tells you that the Prophet concealed part of what was revealed to him is a liar.
Strange is the case of a believer — there is good for him in everything.

famous: Abd Allah ibn Amr ibn al-As
school: Received permission from the Prophet to write down his sayings — producing the Sahifa al-Sadiqa, the earliest recorded hadith collection; known for great piety and asceticism; son of the conqueror Amr ibn al-As
parents: 'Amr bin al-'Aas / Rayta bint Munabbih bin al-Hajjaj
children: Muhammad bin 'Abdullah

school: One of the most prolific Companions; 1,540 hadiths; lived longest of the major Companions; participated in 19 battles with the Prophet
parents: 'Abdullah ibn 'Amr bin Haram / Unaysa bint 'Anma ibn 'Adi
children: 'Abdur Rahman bin Jabir bin 'Abdullah , Umm Habib, 'Aqeel bin Jabir ibn 'Abdullah , 'Abdur Rahman bin Jabir bin 'Abdullah , Mahmud bin Jabir bin 'Abdullah
spouses: Suhayma bint Mas'ud bin Aws
quotes: We were with the Prophet — none of us had any shelter. We built a mosque from palm branches under which we prayed.
The Messenger never refused a request — if he had, he gave or explained.
Love for the Prophet is not only in words — it is in following his way.

school: Cousin of the Prophet — the Prophet prayed for him 'O God, teach him the interpretation'; father of Quranic exegesis; 1,696 hadiths transmitted
parents: al-'Abbas ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib / Umm Fadl Lubaba bint al-Harith
children: 'Ali bin 'Abdullah bin 'Abbas , 'Abbas bin 'Abdullah bin al-Abbas , Muhammad bin 'Abdullah bin 'Abbas , 'Ubaydallah bin 'Abdullah bin al-Abbas , Al-Fadl, Lubabah, Asma
spouses: Zar'ah bint Mishrah b. Madi-Karib
quotes: Take knowledge wherever it comes from — but do not accept the actions of men as religion until you see them in the Quran or Sunnah.
The most beloved of deeds to God is the one that is consistent, even if it is small.
If you want God to give you what you ask, then ask often — for God loves the persistent caller.
Take knowledge wherever it comes from — but do not accept the actions of men as religion until you see them in the Quran or Sunnah.
The most beloved of deeds to God is the one that is consistent, even if it is small.
If you want God to give you what you ask, ask often — God loves the persistent caller.

school: Early Islamic scholarship — one of the few women who taught men in the mosque; gave fatwas; Abu Darda's wife

Son of Ja'far ibn Abi Talib (martyr of Mu'ta) and cousin of Ali; born in Ethiopia during the first hijra; renowned for extraordinary generosity; grew up under the care of the Prophet who declared him like a father to him

First Muslim child born in Medina after the Hijra, whose birth was seen as a good omen; spent his life opposing Umayyad rule; established an independent caliphate from Mecca for nine years; died defending the Ka'ba against Hajjaj ibn Yusuf
parents: Zubayr ibn al-Awwam / Asma' bint Abi Bakr
children: Bakr, Thabit bin 'Abdullah bin al-Zubair , Hamza bin 'Abdullah bin al-Zubair , Khabayb bin 'Abdullah bin al-Zubair , 'Amir bin 'Abdullah , 'Aabad bin 'Abdullah bin al-Zubair , 'Umar bin 'Abdullah bin al-Zubair , Umm 'Amr bint 'Abdullah
quotes: Whoever does not endure the humiliation of learning will endure the humiliation of ignorance.
The strong man is not the one who wrestles others down — the strong man is the one who controls himself in anger.
Do not let the approval of people be your goal — for those who please all people please no one.

school: Youngest daughter of the Prophet Muhammad and Khadijah bint Khuwaylid; wife of Ali ibn Abi Talib and mother of Hasan, Husayn, Zaynab and Umm Kulthum — the sole surviving line through which the Prophet's descendants continue to this day. The Prophet declared her the Sayyidat Nisāʾ al-Janna (foremost woman of Paradise) and rose to greet her whenever she entered a room. She died only six months after her father in 632 CE, still mourning his loss, and was the first of his family to follow him in death.

books.title: Fatimah al-Zahra — World History Encyclopedia
books.note: Modern scholarly overview of her life and significance

quotes: One who worships God sincerely — God will fulfil his needs and relieve his poverty from a source he could never expect.
The best things for women are that they should not see men and men should not see them.

school: Sixth wife of the Prophet Muhammad and the last of the Mothers of the Believers to pass away; she narrated 378 hadiths — the second-highest tally among the Prophet's wives after Aisha. Among the earliest Muslims to migrate to Abyssinia, she endured the forced separation of her husband and infant son during the Hijra. Her counsel at the Treaty of Hudaybiyya, advising the Prophet to lead by example rather than speaking to the companions, averted a potential crisis. The verse of purification (33:33) was revealed in her house.

books.title: Musnad Umm Salamah — Hadith collection
books.note: Compiled collection of her 378 narrations accessible via Sunnah.com

quotes: O Messenger of God, why are men mentioned in the Quran and we are not?
Whoever grieves for the world will find the world is not worth his grief.
The believer’s patience in trial is itself a form of worship.

school: Fourth wife of the Prophet Muhammad and daughter of the second Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab; entrusted by Abu Bakr with the first written compilation of the Quran after the Battle of Yamama, which remained in her home as the master manuscript. Uthman later used her copy as the reference for his standardised Quran. The angel Jibrīl described her to the Prophet as a woman of prayer and fasting who would be his wife in Paradise. She narrated 60 hadiths, with six appearing in both Bukhari and Muslim.

books.title: al-Bukhāri — Ṣaḥīḥ al-Bukhāri
books.note: Contains her narrations including the famous account of the Quranic compilation

quotes: I was entrusted with the written pages of the Quran — and there was no honour greater than guarding the words of God.
The Prophet taught me to seek refuge in God from the punishment of the grave.

school: Among the earliest converts to Islam despite her father Abu Sufyan being the foremost enemy of the Prophet; she migrated to Abyssinia where her husband Ubaydullah ibn Jahsh apostatised. The Negus of Abyssinia conducted her marriage to the Prophet on his behalf as a unique proxy ceremony across the sea. Her steadfast faith in isolation — divorced and abroad — is celebrated as a model of conviction. She narrated 65 hadiths and is ranked third in knowledge among the Mothers of the Believers.
quotes: I chose the Messenger of God over my own father — for the truth is dearer to me than blood.
A woman who prays her five prayers and guards her chastity will enter Paradise from whichever gate she wishes.

school: Elder half-sister of Aisha and daughter of Abu Bakr; she earned her celebrated title by tearing her belt in two to secure provisions for the Prophet and her father hiding in Cave Thawr before the Hijra — doing so while seven months pregnant. One of the first 17-18 Muslims, she defied Abu Jahl's intimidation at her doorstep and later famously exhorted her son Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr to die with honour rather than surrender to al-Hajjaj. She died in 73 AH aged approximately 100, the last surviving Muhājir. Narrated 58 hadiths.

books.title: al-Bukhāri — Ṣaḥīḥ al-Bukhāri (Kitab al-Manaqib)
books.note: Contains the famous 'Two Belts' hadith and key narrations from Asma

quotes: Spend and do not count — for God does not count what He gives you.
The one who fears people more than God has made people his god.
I would rather face hardship with honour than comfort with humiliation.

school: Among the earliest converts to Islam, Asma migrated twice — to Abyssinia with her first husband Jafar ibn Abi Talib, then to Medina. After Jafar's martyrdom at the Battle of Muta (629 CE) she successively married Abu Bakr al-Siddiq and later Ali ibn Abi Talib, making her the sole woman in Islamic history to have been wife to three of the four Rightly Guided Caliphs' closest circle. She was the confidante of Fatimah al-Zahra, washed her body at death per her final wishes, and was present at the most pivotal moments of the Prophetic era.
quotes: We migrated twice — first to Abyssinia and then to Madinah — and we never complained, for the reward of God was greater than any hardship.
I lost my husband in the way of God — and God replaced him with one who was even dearer to me.

school: Anṣārī female companion who accompanied the Prophet on seven military campaigns, nursing the wounded and preparing food. The Prophet personally instructed her in the ritual washing of Zaynab bint Muhammad's body, and her transmission of this procedure became the definitive juristic source across all schools of Islamic law for the funeral bath of women. Al-Ṭabarānī preserved 85 of her traditions; six appear in both Bukhari and Muslim. She later taught in Basra, where Ali ibn Abi Talib visited her.

books.title: al-Bukhāri — Ṣaḥīḥ al-Bukhāri
books.note: Contains her pivotal hadiths on the funeral bath, Eid prayer attendance, and mourning

school: The most eloquent female companion of the Prophet; she famously approached the Prophet as delegate for all Muslim women to raise the question of spiritual equality and reward between men and women — a speech the Prophet praised as surpassing anything he had heard from a woman. She narrated 81 hadiths — the highest count among Anṣārī women. She accompanied the Conquest of Mecca and at the Battle of al-Yarmuk (636 CE) fought with a tent pole, reportedly killing nine Byzantine soldiers. She died in Damascus after a long life of teaching.

Born in Abyssinia during the first Muslim migration, step-daughter of the Prophet through his marriage to her mother Umm Salamah. The Prophet renamed her Zaynab and raised her in his household with great affection — reportedly splashing blessed water on her face in play, a gesture said to have preserved her youthful appearance into old age. She transmitted hadith from Umm Salamah, Aisha, Zaynab bint Jahsh, and Umm Habibah, forming a rare first-hand bridge to the inner household of the Prophet. Eight of her traditions are preserved by al-Ṭabarānī.

Female companion from the Custodians of the Kaaba (Banu Shayba) who narrated traditions directly from several of the Prophet's wives, forming a critical link in the hadith transmission network radiating from the Mothers of the Believers. Narrated from Aisha, Umm Salamah, Umm Habibah and Maymunah, as well as from the companion Asma bint Abi Bakr. Her son Abd al-Hamid ibn Jubayr and grandson Umayyah ibn Safwan were themselves transmitters who carried her traditions to the second generation.

Anṣārī female companion of the Prophet from Medina whose case with Thabit ibn Qays became one of the earliest and most cited precedents in Islamic jurisprudence for the khulʿ — the wife-initiated dissolution of marriage by returning the mahr. She came to the Prophet at dawn to request separation, a case recorded in canonical hadith collections. Her narrative contributed directly to the formation of Islamic family law on women's right to seek divorce.

books.title: al-Bukhāri — Ṣaḥīḥ al-Bukhāri (Kitab al-Talaq)
books.note: Contains the canonical account of her khulʿ case

school: Anṣārī female companion who participated in Bayʿat al-Riḍwān — the Pledge of the Tree at Hudaybiyya praised by a Qurʾānic verse (48:18). She memorised Sūrah Qāf directly from the Prophet Muhammad's repeated recitation of it during his Friday khutba sermons; her account of this is preserved in Ṣaḥīḥ Muslim and became the canonical transmission of that sūrah's oral tradition. A close neighbour of the Prophet, she and Amrah bint Abd al-Rahman both transmitted from her half-sister.

books.title: Muslim — Ṣaḥīḥ Muslim (Kitab al-Jumua)
books.note: Contains her famous account of memorising Sūrat Qāf from the Prophet's Friday sermons

school: Anṣārī female companion from Medina who transmitted traditions directly from the Prophet Muhammad, preserved in the major hadith collections. She is recorded in the biographical dictionaries as a reliable transmitter among the women of the Ansar, and her narrations — including an account of a conversation with the Prophet at Khaybar about the Muslim dead — are cited in the canonical collections. Her son Mubashshir ibn Zayd was also a transmitter of hadith.

A child companion who performed the Farewell Pilgrimage with the Prophet Muhammad at the age of seven, an event he recorded in the canonical hadith of al-Bukhari (no. 1858) — 'I was taken to perform Hajj with the Messenger of Allah when I was seven years old.' As a child companion (min ṣighār al-ṣaḥāba) he lived long enough to transmit traditions to the Tabi'un and became a treasured living link to the Prophetic era; he also served as a market overseer (ṣāḥib al-sūq) in Medina under Umar ibn al-Khattab.

books.title: al-Bukhāri — Ṣaḥīḥ al-Bukhāri (Kitab al-Hajj, Hadith 1858)
books.note: The canonical hadith in which he recounts performing Hajj with the Prophet at age seven

school: Paternal uncle and foster brother of Prophet Muhammad; one of the most formidable warriors in early Islam. Converted to Islam in 616 CE after striking Abu Jahl who had insulted the Prophet, entering Dar al-Arqam immediately after. His conversion was a turning point that gave the early Muslim community courage and strength. He commanded the first Muslim military expedition. At the Battle of Badr (624 CE), he fought with two swords and was credited with decisive individual combat. At Uhud (625 CE), he was treacherously slain by Wahshi ibn Harb, an Abyssinian javelin-thrower hired by Hind bint Utba for revenge. His martyrdom devastated the Prophet, who wept and is narrated to have said that Jibril referred to him as 'the Lion of the Prophet' in the seven heavens. The Prophet called him 'Sayyid al-Shuhada'. His brave death is referenced in Surah al-Nahl (Q16:125-128).
quotes: By God, I will not eat until you take your foot off the neck of my nephew.
I bear witness that Muhammad speaks the truth — and I will defend him with my life.

school: Fourth person to accept Islam; the only companion whose name is explicitly mentioned in the Quran (Surah al-Ahzab 33:37). Originally from the Kalb tribe of Yemen, he was captured as a child and sold into slavery, purchased for Khadijah bint Khuwaylid who gave him as a gift to the Prophet. When his father came to ransom him, Zayd chose to remain with the Prophet, who then publicly adopted him at the Kaaba. He converted to Islam at the very beginning of the mission. The Prophet never sent any army without appointing Zayd as commander (Aisha's testimony). He participated in the Battle of Badr and commanded numerous expeditions. He was martyred at the Battle of Mu'tah (629 CE) commanding a force of 3,000 Muslims against a 200,000-strong Byzantine army. The Prophet wept at his grave, calling him 'the yearning of the lover for the beloved'.
quotes: I would not choose my own father and mother over Muhammad — he is my father and my mother.
The Prophet freed me, but it was Islam that truly set me free.

school: Cousin of Prophet Muhammad and elder brother of Ali ibn Abi Talib; among the earliest converts to Islam. Led the first migration (hijra) to Abyssinia in 616 CE, where he lived for twelve years. Served as the eloquent spokesman for the Muslim community before the Negus (King of Abyssinia), delivering one of the most celebrated early defenses of Islam, reciting the opening of Surah Maryam and moving the Christian king to tears. The Prophet rejoiced at his return from Abyssinia in 628 CE as much as at the conquest of Khaybar. Known for extraordinary generosity to the poor. Martyred at the Battle of Mu'tah (629 CE) while commanding the army after Zayd ibn Harithah fell; he fought until both arms were severed, holding the banner against his chest. The Prophet reported that Jibril said Allah had given Jafar two wings to fly in Paradise in place of his severed arms.
quotes: O King, we were a people of ignorance — we worshipped idols, ate dead animals, committed sins, severed family ties, and the strong among us exploited the weak. Then God sent us a Messenger from among ourselves.
He commanded us to speak the truth, to fulfil our trusts, to be kind to our relatives, and to stop shedding blood.

school: Chief of the Banu Abd al-Ashhal clan of the Aws tribe in Madinah; one of the most pivotal Ansar companions. Converted to Islam in 622 CE (1 AH) through Musab ibn Umayr, whereupon his entire subtribe of Banu Abd al-Ashhal — the first household in Madinah — converted en masse, breaking their idols. His conversion and leadership were critical to the social foundation of the Islamic state in Madinah. He participated in the battles of Badr, Uhud, and the Trench. At Badr he was the standard-bearer of the Aws. At the Battle of the Trench (627 CE), he was struck by an arrow that severed a main vein; he lived long enough to deliver his famous verdict against the treacherous Banu Qurayza, which the Prophet declared was 'the judgment of Allah'. He died of his wounds at age 37. The Prophet stood barefoot at his funeral and declared 'The Throne of the Most Merciful shook at the death of Sa'd ibn Muadh.' Narrated that 70,000 angels attended his funeral prayer.
quotes: O God, if You have decreed war between us and the Quraysh, then keep me alive to fight them. And if You have decreed peace between us, then take my soul.
I judge that the fighting men should be dealt with according to their deeds.

school: Elder half-brother of Umar ibn al-Khattab; converted to Islam before Umar, preceding him in this distinction. Among the earliest Muhajirun who emigrated to Madinah; the Prophet established a bond of brotherhood between him and Ma'n ibn Adi. Participated in the Battle of Badr, Uhud, Khandaq and all campaigns with the Prophet, refusing armour at Uhud so as to be equal with his brother in seeking martyrdom. After the Prophet's death (632 CE), he served as deputy commander under Khalid ibn al-Walid in the Ridda Wars. At the Battle of Yamamah against the false prophet Musaylimah, Zayd served as standard-bearer. When Muslims wavered, he rallied them with a stirring speech, fought until slain, still holding the banner. Umar mourned deeply, saying 'My brother surpassed me in two virtues: he embraced Islam before me and was martyred before me.' His martyrdom alongside many Quran memorisers prompted Umar to urge Abu Bakr to commission the first collection of the Quran.

Persian-born freed slave adopted by Abu Hudhayfah ibn Utba of the Quraysh. Among the earliest Muslim converts; one of four primary Quran teachers personally recommended by the Prophet. The Prophet said: 'Learn the Quran from four persons: Abdullah ibn Masud, Salim Mawla Abi Hudhayfah, Ubayy ibn Kab and Muadh ibn Jabal.' Aisha heard his beautiful Quran recitation one night; the Prophet came to listen and praised Allah for placing someone like Salim in the Ummah. Participated in Badr and all major battles. At the Battle of Yamamah (632 CE) he held the Muslim banner until his right hand was severed, then held it with his left, reciting Quran until martyrdom. Umar, dying years later, said 'If Salim were alive, I would have appointed him my successor.' His death was one of the chief motivations for compiling the Quran into a single written volume.
quotes: I was a freed slave — but the Quran raised me above many free men, for God does not look at your lineage but at your heart.
The Prophet said: take the Quran from four — and he named me among them.

school: Noble Qurayshi companion from Banu Abd Shams; son of the leading Quraysh chieftain Utba ibn Rabi'a. Embraced Islam before the Prophet entered Dar al-Arqam — among the very earliest Muslims. Participated in both migrations to Abyssinia where his son Muhammad was born. Participated in the Battle of Badr where he faced the tragic position of his own father fighting against the Muslims. Fought at Uhud, Khandaq and all major engagements. He adopted Salim, the freed Persian slave who became one of the great Quran teachers. At the Battle of Yamamah (632 CE), he and Salim embraced, made a vow of martyrdom together, shouted 'O People of the Quran!' to rally the Muslims, and both were found slain side by side — one's head at the feet of the other.

Considered the seventh person to embrace Islam. Participated in both migrations to Abyssinia. Tall and impressive, known for exceptional skill with spear and arrow; participated in Badr, Uhud, Khandaq, Yamamah and many campaigns. Under Caliph Umar, he commanded 2,000 men against the Persian garrison at Ubullah (Iraq) in 635 CE, opening lower Mesopotamia to Islam. He founded the garrison city of Basra — one of the great cities of the Islamic world — planning it strategically between the Arabian Desert and the Persian Gulf, building its first mosque. Concerned that Muslims were softening to worldly comforts, he delivered a famous sermon on zuhd (detachment): recalling early Islam when he and Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas shared a single cloak. He asked Umar to relieve him of governorship; Umar refused, and on his return journey to Basra he fell from his camel and died.

Ansari companion from the Aws tribe, Banu Haritha clan; an early Medinan convert. Participated in the Battle of Badr, Uhud and Khandaq. Renowned as the Prophet's most trusted special envoy and special operations commander: assigned delicate, sensitive missions including leadership of the mission against Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf who had incited war against the Muslims with his poetry, as well as numerous other expeditions. He held himself completely apart from the first Fitna (civil war) after Uthman's murder, refusing absolutely to fight any Muslim regardless of faction, maintaining strict neutrality — breaking his sword to symbolise this — a principled stance that distinguished him from virtually all other companions. He died at approximately age 70 in Madinah during the caliphate of Muawiya, one of the last of the Badr companions to die.

One of the Asharah Mubasharah — the Ten Companions explicitly promised Paradise by the Prophet ﷺ. Among the earliest Muslims, converting before the Prophet entered Dar al-Arqam; one of the eight who first accepted Islam. At the Battle of Uhud (625 CE), he shielded the Prophet with his own body when archers broke rank; he sustained 24 wounds and lost the use of two fingers, but kept the Prophet from falling. The Prophet later said of this day: 'Whoever wishes to see a martyr walking the earth, let him look at Talha ibn Ubaydullah.' Known throughout the Muslim community as 'Al-Fayyadh' (the Bountiful) for his extraordinary generosity — he once distributed 700,000 dirhams in a single day to those in need. He fought at Badr, Uhud, and all major campaigns. He was martyred at the Battle of the Camel (36 AH / 656 CE) in Basra, caught in the civil conflict over the caliphate. He is buried in Basra.

The first diplomat in Islamic history and one of the most remarkable transformation stories among the companions. Born to one of the wealthiest Quraysh families, he was famous for his beauty, fine clothes, and luxury — yet he abandoned everything for Islam, converting secretly around 614 CE in the house of al-Arqam. When his family discovered his conversion, they imprisoned and tortured him. He emigrated to Abyssinia. After the First Pledge of Aqabah (621 CE), the Prophet appointed him as Islam's first official envoy to Madinah — charged with teaching Quran, uniting the fractious Aws and Khazraj tribes, and preparing the city for the Prophet's arrival. Within a year, his mission had transformed Madinah: senior tribal chiefs including Sa'd ibn Muadh embraced Islam, and mass conversions followed. He led the first Friday Jumu'ah prayer in Madinah. He carried the Muslim standard at Badr and Uhud, where he was martyred while defending the Prophet. He died so poor that there was only one small cloth to cover him — when placed over his head, his feet showed; when placed over his feet, his head showed. The Prophet wept. He is buried on the battlefield of Uhud.

Early Muslim who emigrated to Abyssinia with the second group of emigrants, seeking refuge from Quraysh persecution before the Hijra to Madinah. Participated in the battles of Uhud. Died as a martyr (625 CE) at Uhud, Hijaz, Uhud.

One of the founding twelve who took the First Pledge of Aqabah (621 CE), the secret pact that initiated the Prophet's migration to Madinah. Participated in the battles of Badr, Uhud. Died 625 CE in Hijaz, Uhud.

One of the founding twelve who took the First Pledge of Aqabah (621 CE), the secret pact that initiated the Prophet's migration to Madinah. Participated in the battles of Badr, Uhud, Khandaq, Ridwan. Served as a judge (Qadhi), among the first judges appointed in Islam.

Ansari companion from Madinah who pledged allegiance to the Prophet ﷺ. Participated in the Battle of Badr (624 CE / 2 AH) — the most decisive early Islamic engagement, in which the Prophet ﷺ prayed: 'O Allah, if this small band perishes, you will not be worshipped on earth.' Being of Badr is the highest distinction among the companions.
One of the founding twelve who took the First Pledge of Aqabah (621 CE), the secret pact that initiated the Prophet's migration to Madinah. Participated in the battles of Uhud, Khandaq.
Companion of the Prophet ﷺ who participated in the Battle of Badr (624 CE / 2 AH). Died 654 CE in Madinah.

One of the Kuttab al-Wahy — the personal scribes of the Quranic revelation appointed by Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Participated in the battles of Badr, Uhud, Khaybar. Died as a martyr (629 CE) at Syria, Mau'ta. Known as a poet among the companions.
quotes: O soul, even if you are not killed, you will die anyway — this is the fate of death that you are receiving.
I do not care in which direction I fall, as long as I fall as a Muslim.

school: Early Muslim and Companion of the Prophet ﷺ who fought at the Battle of Badr (624 CE / 2 AH), the most decisive engagement of early Islam. Died as a martyr (625 CE) at Medina.

famous: Abu Dujana Simak bin Aus bin Kharasha
school: Companion of the Prophet ﷺ who participated in the Battle of Badr (624 CE / 2 AH). Died as a martyr (634 CE) at Hijaz, Yamamah.
quotes: I am the one who pledged to the Prophet that this sword would not strike except in truth.
When the Prophet gave me his sword, I tied the red band around my head and walked between the two ranks.

school: One of the founding twelve who took the First Pledge of Aqabah (621 CE), the secret pact that initiated the Prophet's migration to Madinah. Participated in the battles of Badr, Uhud. Died as a martyr (626 CE) at Hijaz, Bi'r Ma'un.

Ansari companion from Madinah who pledged allegiance to the Prophet ﷺ. Participated in the Battle of Badr (624 CE / 2 AH) — the most decisive early Islamic engagement, in which the Prophet ﷺ prayed: 'O Allah, if this small band perishes, you will not be worshipped on earth.' Being of Badr is the highest distinction among the companions.

Ansari companion from Madinah who pledged allegiance to the Prophet ﷺ. Participated in the Battle of Badr (624 CE / 2 AH) — the most decisive early Islamic engagement, in which the Prophet ﷺ prayed: 'O Allah, if this small band perishes, you will not be worshipped on earth.' Being of Badr is the highest distinction among the companions.
Early Muslim and Companion of the Prophet ﷺ who fought at the Battle of Badr (624 CE / 2 AH), the most decisive engagement of early Islam. Died as a martyr (633 CE) at Hijaz, Yamamah.
Early Muslim and Companion of the Prophet ﷺ who fought at the Battle of Badr (624 CE / 2 AH), the most decisive engagement of early Islam. Died 624 CE in Medinah.
quotes: The world is a distraction from the Hereafter — and whoever busies himself with it loses the greater prize.
I wish I could spend every moment in worship — the world has nothing that interests me.

school: Early Muslim and Companion of the Prophet ﷺ who fought at the Battle of Badr (624 CE / 2 AH), the most decisive engagement of early Islam. Died 650 CE in Medinah. Known as a scholar of Quranic interpretation (Tafsir).

Ansari companion from Madinah who pledged allegiance to the Prophet ﷺ. Participated in the Battle of Badr (624 CE / 2 AH) — the most decisive early Islamic engagement, in which the Prophet ﷺ prayed: 'O Allah, if this small band perishes, you will not be worshipped on earth.' Being of Badr is the highest distinction among the companions.
Companion of the Prophet ﷺ who participated in the Battle of Badr (624 CE / 2 AH). Died as a martyr (634 CE) at Syria, Aynal-Tamr.

Paternal uncle of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, approximately two or three years older than his nephew. He was sympathetic to the Prophet's mission from the beginning but publicly accepted Islam late — some accounts say he was already Muslim secretly but maintained his position among the Quraysh to protect Muslims. He was captured at the Battle of Badr fighting on the Quraysh side; the Prophet freed him after he paid a large ransom. He formally embraced Islam at the time of the Conquest of Makkah (630 CE / 8 AH) and thereafter distinguished himself. He participated at the Battle of Hunayn and gave a pivotal rallying call when the Muslims began to flee, shouting out to the companions — his powerful voice turning the rout into a renewed charge. After the Prophet's death, he was one of the most respected figures in the community. He died at age approximately 88 in Madinah. The Abbasid Caliphate (750–1258 CE), which ruled the Islamic world for five centuries, descended from him.
quotes: A man who has no shame has no faith.
I never abandoned the Messenger of God in any hardship — and I was the one who held his stirrup at Hunayn.

Elder brother of Ali ibn Abi Talib and son of Abu Talib. He was approximately twenty years older than Ali. Known as the greatest genealogist of the Quraysh — the Prophet frequently consulted him on matters of tribal lineage. He embraced Islam after the Conquest of Makkah (630 CE). He participated in the Battle of Hunayn and subsequent campaigns. He was blind in old age and lived during the caliphate of Ali, but maintained a complex relationship with his brother's rule, eventually residing at Muawiyah's court in Damascus due to financial difficulties. He narrated hadith and is a transmitter in various chains. His descendants include many important figures in later Islamic history. He died during the caliphate of Muawiyah (c. 670 CE).

Companion of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ present at the Pledge of Ridwan (Bay'at al-Ridwan) at Hudaybiyya in 628 CE / 6 AH. Ka'b ibn Zuhayr was an Arab poet and companion of Prophet Muhammad. His poem 'Banat Su'ad' (Su'ad Has Departed), recited before the Prophet, is one of the most celebrated odes in Arabic literature. The Prophet reportedly gave Ka'b his mantle (burda) as a reward, and the poem became known as the original 'Qasidat al-Burda,' inspiring al-Busiri's later masterpiece.

Abdullah ibn Salam was a Jewish rabbi of Medina from the Banu Qaynuqa tribe who converted to Islam upon the Prophet Muhammad's arrival. He is praised in hadith literature for his piety and knowledge of the Torah. Islamic tradition holds that the Quranic verse 'a witness from the Children of Israel testified to something similar' (46:10) refers to him.
